coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive detection of Cr2O7 2− in farmland achieved by an electrosynthesized fluorescent poly(Fmoc-succinimide)
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fei
Duan, Xuemin
Hu, Shuxin
Zhang, Long
Shen, Liang
Liu, Fang
Li, Hui
Zhang, Ge
Xu, Jingkun
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) pollution is related to human health, and the detection of its concentration has a decisive effect on pollution treatment. In this work, we designed a Cr2O7 2− sensor P(Fmoc-Osu) and applied it in farmland. In the boron trifluoride diethyl etherate solution, the monomer Fmoc-Osu was electropolymerized and obtained its conjugated polymer P(Fmoc-Osu). The introduction of succinimide and carbonic ester group in side chain enabled it to show high selectivity to Cr2O7 2− in comparison to anions and twenty amino acids. Furthermore, by taking advantage of the signal amplification effect of a conjugated polymer, P(Fmoc-Osu) could analyze Cr2O7 2− in trace amounts and the limit of detection was 8.3 fM. For agricultural application, we employed it in farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage, carrot and the results showed a satisfactory result. All these results showed that P(Fmoc-Osu) is an efficient tool to detect Cr2O7 2− in practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cr2O7 2−
Fluorescent sensor
Conjugated polymer
Ultra-sensitive detection
Farmland

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs of Fmoc-Osu in the BFEE solvent system on a Pt working electrode. Potential scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Inset: electrosynthesis of P(Fmoc-Osu).

Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of P(Fmoc-Osu) (22 μM) in the presence of various anions (0.4 mM) in DMSO/PBS (1:99, v/v) solution. F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of solution with or without ions, respectively. Ex = 335 nm.

Fluorescence intensity of P(Fmoc-Osu) upon the addition of 0.1 mM Cr2O7 2− in DMSO-PBS (v/v = 1:99) solution contain 0.1 mM various anions (a) and natural amino acids (b). Black bar: P(Fmoc-Osu) + competing anions. Red bar: P(Fmoc-Osu) + competing anions + Cr2O7 2−. Ex = 335 nm.

The fluorescence spectra (a) of P(Fmoc-Osu) toward Cr2O7 2− with different concentrations ((1) blank; (2) 2.5 × 10−14 M; (3) 5.0 × 10−14 M; (4) 2.5 × 10−12 M; (5) 2.5 × 10−11 M; (6) 1.0 × 10−8 M; (7) 2.5 × 10−6 M; (8) 2.5 × 10−5 M; (9) 4.0 × 10−5 M; (10) 7.5 × 10−5 M; (11) 9.5 × 10−5 M; (12) 1.0 × 10−4 M; (13) 1.5 × 10−4 M; (14) 3.0 × 10−4 M; (15) 4.0 × 10−4 M) in DMSO/PBS (1:99, v/v) solution and linear plots (b) of the fluorescence intensity against Cr2O7 2− concentration. Fmoc-Osu: 22 μM, Ex = 335 nm.
TEM of P(Fmoc-Osu) (a) and P(Fmoc-Osu) with Cr2O7 2− (b).
The fabrication of Cr2O7 2− sensor based on P(Fmoc-Osu) and its application in farmland (water, soil, Chinese cabbage, and carrot).

Parameters and performance of different Cr2O7 2− sensors based on different materials (metal-organic frameworks, organic small molecules, ionic liquid, carbon dots, quantum dots, nanoclusters, coordination polymer and conjugated polymers) in reports of the last four years.

row.entry: In real samples from the tap water, the Yellow River and the steam
In the local Zhujiang River water and drinking water
In the different color rice sample
In the farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage and carrot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Cr2O7 2− in agro-product samples solution using P(Fmoc-Osu).

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive detection of Cr2O7 2− in farmland achieved by an electrosynthesized fluorescent poly(Fmoc-succinimide)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Science & Technology Normal University, Nanchang, 330013, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) pollution is related to human health, and the detection of its concentration has a decisive effect on pollution treatment. In this work, we designed a Cr2O7 2− sensor P(Fmoc-Osu) and applied it in farmland. In the boron trifluoride diethyl etherate solution, the monomer Fmoc-Osu was electropolymerized and obtained its conjugated polymer P(Fmoc-Osu). The introduction of succinimide and carbonic ester group in side chain enabled it to show high selectivity to Cr2O7 2− in comparison to anions and twenty amino acids. Furthermore, by taking advantage of the signal amplification effect of a conjugated polymer, P(Fmoc-Osu) could analyze Cr2O7 2− in trace amounts and the limit of detection was 8.3 fM. For agricultural application, we employed it in farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage, carrot and the results showed a satisfactory result. All these results showed that P(Fmoc-Osu) is an efficient tool to detect Cr2O7 2− in practical application.

An ultra-sensitive fluorescent sensor P(Fmoc-Osu) could directly detect Cr2O7 2− in farmland.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is a global concern and should be given high attention [1–3]. The pollution of the farmland environment, such as water and soil, will affect the growth of crops and reduce the quality and yield of crops [4–6]. Especially, heavy metals can accumulate in grains and enter the food chain, which will cause damage to animal and human health [7]. Compared to other heavy-metal ions, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) pollution is more commonly due to its excellent water solubility in the whole pH range [8–10]. Nevertheless, Cr(VI) is widely used in our daily life. So World Health Organization (WHO) strictly requires the content Cr(VI) not to exceed 50 μg/L in drinking water [11], 5–250 mg/kg in different foodstuffs, and chromium concentrations in food plants growing on normal soils range from not detectable to 0.19 mg/kg [12]. Thus, monitoring the content of Cr(VI) in farmland is essential and has great significance for human health [13,14].

As an advanced analytical method, fluorescence analysis solves the problems of the traditional detection method for Cr(VI) [15]. This method is simple, low cost, highly sensitive, and fast response [16–18]. Thus, fluorescent sensors based on various materials were designed, such as metal-organic frameworks [19–27], organic small molecules [28], ionic liquid [29], carbon dots [30], quantum dots [31,32], nanoclusters [33–35], coordination polymer [36] and conjugated polymers [37] (Table 1 ). Indeed, the fluorescent sensors show good performance for detecting Cr2O7 2− in real samples, such as water samples and bioimaging. Unfortunately, few efforts have been devoted to the development of fluorescent sensors for the analysis of Cr2O7 2− in farmland, which is closely related to human health. In fact, conjugated polymers (CPs) are a good choice for constructing fluorescent sensors with excellent properties because CPs can amplify the fluorescent response signal and realize a completely novel sensing model compared with other fluorescent materials [38,39]. Previously, we have used a CP to fabricate the fluorescent sensor, which showed a high sensitivity for detecting Cr2O7 2− in different color rice [37]. The success of our previous work shows that CPs are very promising for fabricating excellent fluorescent sensors. In other word, the fluorescent sensors for the analysis of Cr2O7 2− in farmland based on CPs are very precious but lack.

So we continually employed CPs to fabricate fluorescent sensors to ultra-trace detect Cr2O7 2− in farmland, ensuring the high sensitivity of sensors in this work. The fluorene unit was selected because of its high fluorescence quantum yield, good optical stability, and good electrochemical activity. Especially, the C9 of fluorine can be easily modified by special functional groups. The introduction of succinimide and carbonic ester groups with N and O atoms in this work was aimed to recognize Cr2O7 2−. Fmoc-Osu was electropolymerized in boron trifluoride diethyl etherate (BFEE) and the obtained polymer P(Fmoc-Osu) showed high selectivity to Cr2O7 2− among common anions and natural amino acids. Especially, this sensor has ultra-high sensitivity to Cr2O7 2− and the limit of detection (LOD) is 8.3 fM, which shows the lowest detection limit reported so far. The successful design of P(Fmoc-Osu) enables it to be applied in farmland (Scheme 1 ).

N-(9-Fluorenylmethoxycarbonyloxy)succinimide (Fmoc-Osu, 98%, Aladdin) (Beijing Balinway Technology Co. LTD) were used directly. Boron trifluoride diethyl etherate (BFEE) (Beijing Changyang Chemical Plant, China) was distilled and stored at −20 °C before use. Twice distilled water was used throughout all experiments. The aqueous solution of Sn2+ was prepared from its chloride salt; the aqueous solution of Ag+ was prepared from its perchloric acid salt; aqueous solutions of Sr2+, Ga3+, Pd2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, K+, Cr3+, Al3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, and In3+ were prepared from their nitrate salts. The aqueous solution of Cr2O7 2− was prepared from its kalium salt, aqueous solutions of F−, CNO−, HS−, CH3COO−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, HCO3 2−, NO2 −, Br−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, PO4 3−, SCN− and HSO3 − were prepared from their sodium salts. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), hydrochloric acid, and aqueous ammonia were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Plant. The farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage, and carrot were obtained from the experimental field in Jiangxi Agricultural University.

Electrochemical polymerization was performed using a Model 263 A potentiostat-galvanostat (EG&G Princeton Applied Research) under computer control. GPC measurements of P(Fmoc-Osu) were studied using America Waters 1525 Gel chromatograph in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). Infrared spectra were measured in a broker Vertex 70 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AV-400NMR spectrometer at a room temperature, and tetramethylsilane was used as the internal standard to measure the chemical shifts. Absorption spectra were obtained from an Agilent 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer. All fluorescent experiments were studied on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorospectrophotometer with excitation/emission slit set at 5 nm.

We still used a three electrodes system to prepare P(Fmoc-Osu). Two platinum wires were the counter and working electrodes, and Ag/AgCl was the reference electrode [40]. In order to obtain a large number of polymer films, the working and counter electrodes were replaced with two rectangular ITO whose surface area was 4 cm2. And the film formation potential was 1.26 V. For fluorescent sensing, the electrolyte and monomer must be removed, so the polymer ﬁlms were washed repeatedly with anhydrous diethyl ether. Then these films were dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 24 h [37]. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was used to measure the molecular weight of P(Fmoc-Osu) (Mw = 67,460, Mn = 25,652, PDI = 2.63).

P(Fmoc-Osu) was first dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and then diluted in DMSO-PBS (pH = 7) (v/v = 1:99) mixture (2.2 × 10−3 M). For fluorescent sensing, the Cr2O7 2− solutions with different concentrations were added into the solution of P(Fmoc-Osu), and the P(Fmoc-Osu) final concentration in the cuvette was 2.2 × 10−5 M.

We used the standard addition method to evaluate the applicability of P(Fmoc-Osu) in real samples. The farmland water and soil were obtained from the campus at Jiangxi Agricultural University. The Chinese cabbage and carrot were obtained from a supermarket in Nanchang. Cr2O7 2− with different concentrations was added into these samples by a pipette. Then all sample solutions with Cr2O7 2− or without Cr2O7 2− were detected using P(Fmoc-Osu).

Some reports and our previous work have proved that BFEE can facilitate the formation of high-quality polymer film for fluorene and its derivatives through the electrochemical method because BFEE can reduce the oxidation potential of fluorene. The monomer Fmoc-Osu could dissolve in pure BFEE, so we synthesized its polymer P(Fmoc-Osu) in this system. We studied successive cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of P(Fmoc-Osu), which could reflect its electrochemical behavior. From the first cycle of CVs in Fig. 1 , we can see that the oxidation potential of Fmoc-Osu was 1.06 V (Fig. S1). And redox wave current densities increased as CV scanning continued. Meanwhile, the brown color film on the working electrode was observed. All these results indicated that Fmoc-Osu could polymerize in the BFEE system.

To investigate the polymerization sites of the monomer, the FT-IR spectra of the monomer and the corresponding polymer were studied (Fig. S2). 3396 cm−1, 3218 cm−1, and 1703 cm−1 belonged to CO of succinimide group stretch vibration peaks. The peaks at 1080 cm−1 and 1217 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–O–C of carbonic ester group stretch vibration peak. And the monomer and the polymer showed very similar IR absorptions in the range of 1100–1600 cm−1 being associated with C–N stretch. These peaks can be clearly observed in both monomer and polymer, indicating the stable existence of the side chain before and after polymerization. The absorption peaks at 822 cm−1, 773 cm−1, and 736 cm−1 belonged to C–H bending modes in the plane of the 1,2,4-disubstituted benzene ring of fluorine, which indicated that the polymerization mainly occurred at 2, 7 position [41,42].

In order to get deep insight into the polymer structure and the polymerization mechanism of fluorine, the 1H NMR spectra of the Fmoc-Osu and the P(Fmoc-Osu) obtained from pure BFEE were recorded. As shown in Fig. S3a, the proton chemical shifts from ca. 7.31 to 7.35, 7.38 to 7.42, 7.82 to 7.84, and 7.86 to 7.88 can be ascribed to the protons at the (C(3), C(6)), (C(2), C(7)), (C(4), C(5)), and (C(1), C(8)) [43]. These proton lines were much broader than the corresponding proton lines of Fmoc-Osu because of the wide molar mass distribution of P(Fmoc-Osu) (Fig. S3b). The chemical shifts at 7.38 to 7.42 could be ascribed to the protons at C2 and C7 positions, which disappeared in the spectrum of P(Fmoc-Osu). The proton peak at δ = 3.3 in the monomer spectrum belongs to the H at the C (9), and the corresponding position in the polymer spectrum also appeared, which proved that the polymerization site occurs on the benzene ring [44].

Generally, the polymers of aromatic compounds with rigid rings and conjugated chains can hardly dissolve in water or organic solvents. In this work, the introduction of a side chain in P(Fmoc-Osu) can make the polymer film completely dissolve in DMSO, DMF, and tetrahydrofuran. But the film only partly dissolved in water and ethanol. For further application, we chose the DMSO-PBS (pH = 7.0) (v/v = 1:99) mixture solution as the detection system. We first tested the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the monomer and the polymer and the results were displayed in Fig. S4. The monomer Fmoc-Osu showed two absorbance peaks at 228 nm and 255 nm, which were the characteristic absorption peaks of fluorene. The polymer P(Fmoc-Osu) only had one absorption peak that shifted to 335 nm. When excited by 255 nm and 335 nm, the monomer and the polymer showed the emission peaks at 325 nm and 420 nm, respectively. This indicated that the polymer P(Fmoc-Osu) was a blue emitter. Also, the red-shifts of the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the polymer showed that the conjugated chain length of P(Fmoc-Osu) increased.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the sensor to Cr2O7 2−, selective experiments of some representative cations and anions were studied. As shown in Fig. 2 , the common cations and anions were added to the sensor, respectively, and the only Cr2O7 2− caused fluorescence quenching which quenched about 90% fluorescence of P(Fmoc-Osu). Compared with the sensor (PFOLS) reported by our group, P(Fmoc-Osu) showed higher selectivity to Cr2O7 2−, which did not require the addition of EDTA to exclude Cu2+ and Fe3+.

Competitive experiments could better evaluate the reliability of sensors in complex environments [45]. In the presence of other anions, Cr2O7 2− could still quench the fluorescence of P(Fmoc-Osu) (Fig. 3 a), which showed that P(Fmoc-Osu) detecting Cr2O7 2− could not be affected by these anions. Also, the interference of amino acids which exist in plant or foods were eliminated in order to apply it in agriculture (Fig. 3b).

In the DMSO-PBS mixture (v/v = 1:99), we carried out the Cr2O7 2− titration experiments of P(Fmoc-Osu). As depicted in Fig. 4 a, after adding Cr2O7 2− to P(Fmoc-Osu) solution, fluorescence quenching was immediately observed. Then two calibration curve was constructed by plotting the concentration of lg [Cr2O7 2−] versus the fluorescent intensities (I) (Fig. 4b). One calibration curve was linear in the concentration ranges 0.025 pM-25 μM, ((y1 = 436.57–49.6 × 1 (R1 2 = 0.9672)) and the other was linear in the concentration ranges 45 μM-0.4 mM((y2 = −1779.33 – 539.07 × 2 (R2 2 = 0.9597)), indicating that P(Fmoc-Osu) could detect the content of Cr2O7 2− in practical sample. The limit of detection (LOD) of Cr2O7 2− was 8.3 fM, suggesting that P(Fmoc-Osu) was very sensitive to Cr2O7 2−. In comparison to other Cr2O7 2− sensors displayed in Table 1, the LOD of P(Fmoc-Osu) was lower and the Cr2O7 2− concentration detection range was wider, indicating that P(Fmoc-Osu) is more flexible in practical application. According to the previous work, the usual quenching mechanism of the P(Fmoc-Osu) based fluorescent sensor was analyte-induced aggregation of polymer chains in solution [40]. As shown in TEM of Fig. 5 a, in solution, polymer chains were freely distributed. After addition of Cr2O7 2−, they could interact with the introduction of succinimide and carbonic ester groups with N and O atoms, which caused the formation of inter- and intra-polymer aggregation and this resulted in the fluorescence self-quenching of P(Fmoc-Osu) [46].

We detected Cr2O7 2− in the farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage, and carrot using P(Fmoc-Osu) for evaluating its feasibility. As listed in Table 2 , all the sample solutions without Cr2O7 2− did not cause the observable fluorescence quenching, which showed that these samples might not contain Cr2O7 2−. Then P(Fmoc-Osu) was employed to detect Cr2O7 2− in practical sample solutions by the standard addition method. The calculated recovery values (94.0%–112.0%) and RSD (0.02%–0.11%) values in these real samples were very close to that in water samples, showing that P(Fmoc-Osu) could easily detect the unknown Cr2O7 2− content in farmland.

In summary, we designed an ultra-highly sensitive Cr2O7 2− sensor P(Fmoc-Osu) by taking advantage of the signal amplification effect of conjugated polymers. By carefully designing the side chain, P(Fmoc-Osu) showed high selectivity to Cr2O7 2− which could easily exclude the interferences of anions and natural amino acids. Combining our expertise, we successfully employed this sensor to detect Cr2O7 2− in farmland water, soil, Chinese cabbage and carrot. All these showed that P(Fmoc-Osu) has the potential to be promoted and used in various fields.

Fei Li: Conceptualization, Experimentation, Writing – review & editing. Xuemin Duan: Resources, Investigation. Shuxin Hu: Formal analysis. Long Zhang: Formal analysis. Liang Shen: Data curation. Fang Liu: Methodology. Hui Li: Investigation. Ge Zhang: Project administration, Validation. Jingkun Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52073128, 51762020), Jiangxi Province Technology Innovation Guidance Project (20203AEI003), and Scientific Research Foundation for Doctors in Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University (2017BSQD006)，and Projects for Postgraduate Innovation in Jiangxi (YC2020-X29).
